title: Energy in a capacitor: the area under the voltage–charge line
scenario: A $C = 500\ \mu$F capacitor is charged until it holds $Q = 0.1$ C, reaching $V = 200$ V. How much energy is stored — and why is it $\tfrac12 CV^2$ and not $CV^2$? As charge piles on, the voltage climbs as $V = q/C$; the energy is the area under that rising line. Drag the cursor and watch the stored energy grow as the triangle under $V(q)$.

assumptions.claim: Ideal capacitor: fixed capacitance $C$, no leakage, charge spread uniformly so $V = q/C$ holds throughout.
The 'where did half the energy go' step assumes the charge is delivered through some resistance (any real wire); the dissipated half does not depend on how small that resistance is.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you
steps.latex: U = \tfrac12 C V^2 = \tfrac12 Q V \qquad(\bar V = \tfrac12 V,\ \text{the average voltage})
steps.prose: The energy in a capacitor is handed to you as $\tfrac12 C V^2$, usually justified by an *average voltage*: the voltage ramps from $0$ to $V$, so on average the charge crosses $\tfrac12 V$, giving $U = \tfrac12 Q V$. That works — but only because the voltage rises *linearly*, and the algebra cannot say why the factor is $\tfrac12$ rather than $1$.

energy_stored.label: Energy stored $U = \tfrac12 C V^2 = Q^2/2C$ (the shaded area)

battery_work.label: If the voltage were constant: $W = VQ$ (the rectangle — twice the energy)

steps.label: Energy is the area under the voltage–charge line
steps.prose: Pushing a charge $dq$ across the capacitor's voltage $V$ costs energy $V\,dq$. The total stored energy is the integral of voltage over charge — the area under the $V$–$q$ graph. Because $V = q/C$ rises from zero, that area is a triangle, not a rectangle.

steps.label: Evaluate for the linear law $V(q) = q/C$
steps.prose: The area under a straight line from the origin is $\tfrac12\,(\text{base})\,(\text{height}) = \tfrac12\,Q\,(Q/C) = Q^2/2C$. Substituting $Q = CV$ gives the memorized $\tfrac12 C V^2$ — it is exactly the area, and its slope is $V(q)$.

steps.label: Where the other half went
steps.latex: \boxed{\,U = \tfrac12 V Q \quad\text{but a battery does}\quad W = V Q\,}
steps.prose: If a battery had held the voltage at $V$ the whole time, it would have done $W = VQ$ — the full rectangle. The capacitor stores only the triangle $\tfrac12 VQ$; the other half is dissipated as heat in the wire's resistance while the charge flows. The constant-voltage rectangle is the quadrature; the real charging is the area under a rising line.

proof.heading: The stored energy is the area under the voltage–charge line — and the memorized ½CV² falls out of the integral.
proof.detail: check $\tfrac{dU}{dq} = V$; check $U = \int_0^q V\,dq'$; recover $\tfrac12 C V^2$; the constant-voltage case is the rectangle $VQ$ (twice the energy)

checks.claim: The energy's slope is the voltage: $U'(q) = V(q)$ — the area's rate of growth is the curve's height.
The stored energy is exactly the area: $U(q) = \int_0^q V\,dq'$.
The memorized $\tfrac12 CV^2$ is the area at full charge: $\tfrac12 C V^2 = U(Q)$ with $V = Q/C$.
A battery holding the voltage constant would do $W = VQ$ — a rectangle, twice the stored energy (the quadrature).

misconception.claim: Charging a capacitor to voltage $V$ with a battery of EMF $V$ stores all the energy the battery delivers.
misconception.refuted_by: The battery delivers $W = VQ$ (it moves charge $Q$ across a fixed voltage $V$ — a *rectangle*). But the capacitor's voltage rises from zero as it fills, so it stores only the *triangle* under the $V$–$q$ line, $\tfrac12 VQ = \tfrac12 CV^2$. Exactly half the delivered energy is lost as heat in the charging resistance — and that fraction is independent of the resistance. The factor of $\tfrac12$ is the area of a triangle, not a fudge.

practice.asks: the energy stored in the capacitor
practice.prompt: A $C = 500\,\mu$F capacitor is charged to $V = 200$ V. How much energy does it store?

choices.misconception: Used $CV^2 = QV$ — the work the battery does. Half of that is dissipated in the resistance while charging; the capacitor stores only $\tfrac12 CV^2$.
Forgot to square the voltage. The stored energy is $\tfrac12 CV^2$, not $\tfrac12 CV$.

algebra_steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you
algebra_steps.latex: U = \tfrac12 C V^2 = \tfrac12 Q V \qquad(\bar V = \tfrac12 V,\ \text{the average voltage})

calculus_steps.label: Where the other half went
calculus_steps.latex: \boxed{\,U = \tfrac12 V Q \quad\text{but a battery does}\quad W = V Q\,}
calculus_steps.prose: If a battery had held the voltage at $V$ the whole time, it would have done $W = VQ$ — the full rectangle. The capacitor stores only the triangle $\tfrac12 VQ$; the other half is dissipated as heat in the wire's resistance while the charge flows. The constant-voltage rectangle is the quadrature; the real charging is the area under a rising line.

practice.asks: the work done by the battery
practice.prompt: Charging the same $C = 500\,\mu$F capacitor to $V = 200$ V, how much work does the **battery** do (which is more than the energy the capacitor ends up storing)?

choices.misconception: That is the energy **stored** in the capacitor, $\tfrac12 CV^2$. The battery does $QV = CV^2$ — *twice* as much; the other half is dissipated while charging.
Doubled it. The battery moves charge $Q$ through a steady potential $V$, doing $QV$ — there is no extra factor of $2$.
